schools.summary: Matthew Henry weighs the first entrance of Nebuchadnezzar, the head of gold, whose name enters Scripture only as the destroyer of Jerusalem, and draws the moral that Jehoiakim's servitude to Babylon was the fruit of not serving his God.
Gill sets the first subjugation in the latter end of Jehoiakim's third or beginning of his fourth year, when Daniel and the temple vessels were carried off.
Clarke traces Nebuchadnezzar's rise from Carchemish, reducing Jehoiakim from a tributary of Necho to a tributary of Babylon until his revolt three years later.
Ellicott dates the humbling to Jehoiakim's fifth or sixth year, after Carchemish and Nabopolassar's death, with the king put in chains and the treasures despoiled.

debates.question: In which year of Jehoiakim did Nebuchadnezzar first subdue him?

sides.position: the third or fourth year, when Daniel and the first captives were carried off

schools.summary: Ellicott sets the raiding bands as Egypt-instigated reprisals by Judah's 'evil neighbours' (Jer. 12:8-17), while Keil frames the whole advance from Daniel's third year, the taking of Jerusalem, and the first deportation.
Clarke gathers the prophetic warnings behind the invasion (Huldah, Jeremiah), and Wesley notes the bands were of several nations serving under a mighty emperor.
Henry stresses that only the commission from the King of kings is named — the LORD sent the bands — so that men serve God's purposes unawares.
Gill notes the Lord sent against Jehoiakim by the hand of the Nebuchadnezzar against whom he had rebelled.

Ellicott renders 'only upon the mouth of Jehovah' (with the LXX/Syriac 'wrath' variant) and Keil grounds the removal in Manasseh's sins as the full, unforgivable measure that not even Moses and Samuel could avert.
Gill reads the verse as God's sure decree of captivity, for which he stirred up the spirit of Nebuchadnezzar.
Wesley insists Judah was punished properly for their own sins, and only occasionally for Manasseh's, which they made their own by repetition.

debates.question: For whose sins was Judah removed out of God's sight?

sides.position: for the sins of Manasseh, the filled-up measure of a long provocation
properly for their own sins, Manasseh's only as they repeated them

themes: at the commandment of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott refuses to soften 'Jehovah willed not to pardon,' making Manasseh's innocent blood the crying, cumulative guilt that sealed the doom, and defends the Old Testament doctrine that children bore the fathers' iniquity until Ezekiel.
Gill sees in the innocent blood the long, cruel usage of the prophets and servants of the Lord.

outliers.note: Would soften vv.3-4 to mean Judah was punished not for Manasseh's guilt but only for persisting in the same kind of sins — a reading Ellicott rejects.
Guesses the 'innocent blood' means the murder of some prominent person such as Isaiah, distinguishing the two clauses — which Ellicott denies on the parallel of 2 Kings 21:16.

schools.summary: Ellicott gathers from Habakkuk and Jeremiah that Jehoiakim oppressed his people with forced labour on Jerusalem's defences, and marks this as the last citation of the royal chronicles, which Bähr thinks did not extend beyond his reign.
Gill draws from 2 Chronicles the added 'abominations, and that which was found in him,' interpreted (with Lyra) of idolatrous body-markings.

themes: the last of the royal annals

schools.summary: Keil harmonizes 'slept with his fathers' with Jeremiah's ass-burial prophecy, and Ellicott, noting the pointedly omitted burial, judges Jehoiakim slain by the raiding bands (with Ewald's conjecture of a treacherous seizure) and his body cast out.
Wesley affirms the fulfilment of Jeremiah's prophecy that Jehoiakim would be buried with the burial of an ass; Clarke turns to the vassal-successor Jehoiachin, scarcely to be reckoned a king.
Gill says Jehoiakim died but had no burial, bound in chains and cast out at the gates of Jerusalem per the prophecy.

sides.position: a violent death, the body dishonoured and left unburied, fulfilling Jeremiah's prophecy (Ewald: lured out and treacherously slain)
the text asserts merely his death, not necessarily a violent one

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read the verse as the seal on Egypt's collapse — Necho stripped of all from the Wady-el-Arish to the Euphrates — leaving Judah denuded of any external help before the coming siege.
Gill notes Necho came no more to gather tribute or help Judah's kings until Zedekiah's day, and then only to retire.
Clarke says Necho was so crushed he was confined to his own states and could attempt no more conquest.

themes: from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates
the reward of a hopeful revolt

schools.summary: Wesley holds firmly to a co-regency (eight with his father, eighteen alone); Clarke rejects harmonizing and calls the 'eight' of Chronicles a plain textual error, since a boy could not have wives or 'do evil' as a king.
Henry laments the tragic brevity — a prince who came to the crown only to lose it, thrust into a falling house, who yet smarted for his fathers' sins because he trod in their steps.
Gill offers both a co-regency and Lightfoot's chronology, but inclines finally to a copyist's slip of eight for eighteen (so the Arabic and Syriac).
Ellicott sets aside the arithmetic to dwell on the names Jehoiachin, Jeconiah, and the ominous Coniah, and on his mother Nehushta and grandfather Elnathan.

debates.question: How is Jehoiachin's age reconciled — eighteen here, eight in 2 Chron. 36:9?

sides.position: a co-regency: eight when he began to reign with his father, eighteen when he reigned alone
a textual error in Chronicles, to be corrected to eighteen

themes: a crown lost as soon as won

schools.summary: Keil registers the verdict and moves to the siege at 'the turn of the year'; Ellicott canvasses whether Ezekiel's young lion denotes Jehoiachin and corrects Josephus's praise of him as a misreading of Jeremiah.
Gill explains the evil from the co-regency: partner with his father on the throne, Jehoiachin was partner in his sins.

debates.question: Does the young lion of Ezekiel 19:5-9 denote Jehoiachin?

sides.position: no — Zedekiah, since so brief a reign cannot bear the image

outliers.note: Calls Jehoiachin 'naturally good and just,' against the verdict of Scripture — a judgment Ellicott ascribes to a misunderstanding of Jeremiah 22:24, 28.

themes: judgment not lengthened by the reign

schools.summary: Ellicott sets the siege in the spring, reports Thenius's cordon across the south to cut off Egypt, and glosses 'servants' as generals and 'was besieged' as looking on to the final siege.
Henry hears in the encircling of the city the fulfilment of the covenant curse of Deut. 28:49 — a fierce nation brought from far to besiege them in their gates.
Gill fixes the moment simply: 'at that time,' when Jehoiachin reigned.
Ellicott corrects the tenses ('were besieging,' 'came unto'), the king arriving after the siege was begun; Keil explains he came in person to punish Jehoiakim's revolt in the person of his successor.
Gill notes Nebuchadnezzar came in person, following his army.

themes: the terror of the mighty at the gates

schools.summary: Keil traces Jehoiachin's surrender in hope of favour, met instead as a rebel and led captive with his household per Jer. 22:24; Ellicott dates the taking to Nebuchadnezzar's eighth year, tallying with Jeremiah.
Clarke sees a prudent surrender for better terms; Wesley calls it the counsel of Jeremiah and to the king's own good, yet faults his want of an Israelite's faith and resolution.
Gill: the young king went out not to fight but to submit and cast himself on the conqueror's mercy.
Henry laments a failure of faith — had he done as Hezekiah did, he need not have feared Babylon; wanting piety, he had not the resolution of a man.

debates.question: Was Jehoiachin's surrender prudent submission or a failure of faith?

sides.position: a wise and necessary submission to an unassailable power
a cowardly forsaking of the God who could have defended him

schools.summary: Keil explains 'cut in pieces' as stripping the gold plating (cf. 2 Kings 16:17); Ellicott finds the surviving treasure evidence of secret store-chambers and hears the fulfilment of the word to Hezekiah (2 Kings 20:17).
Clarke counts three spoilings of the temple by Nebuchadnezzar (under Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, Zedekiah); Wesley marvels the golden vessels had survived earlier plunder only to be swept away now.
Gill pictures the gates thrown open and both temple and palace stripped of their riches.
Henry seizes on the fulfilment of Isaiah's word that all should be carried to Babylon, the vessels reserved for Belshazzar's profane feast.

Keil takes 'all Jerusalem' as the best portion of the people (the good figs of Jer. 24); Ellicott explains the craftsmen and smiths as those whose removal disarmed the city, dismissing Kimchi's fancy that 'smiths' were riddling scholars.
Clarke and Wesley read the 'mighty men of valour' as fighting men, distributing the ten thousand into seven thousand warriors, a thousand smiths, and two thousand princes and officers.
Gill takes 'all Jerusalem' to mean the chief, rich, and useful inhabitants, the poorer sort being left.
Henry reads the deportation as policy and providence — the strength of the city carried off to weaken and enrich, among them Ezekiel and later Mordecai.

debates.question: Who were the deported 'mighty men of valour'?

sides.position: the fighting men, the nation's soldiery

outliers.note: Explains the 'smiths' (those who shut) as learned men who shut others' mouths with riddles none can solve — a reading Ellicott calls a curiosity of exegesis.

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the differing verb ('yea, he carried away'), defines the royal captives, and sees the exact fulfilment of Jer. 22:24-27 over Coniah; Keil treats it as the clearing of the throne for a puppet-king.
Gill marks that Jehoiachin continued in Babylon at least thirty-seven years.

Ellicott works the arithmetic of the ten thousand against the 3,023 of Jer. 52:28 and the phrase 'strong and apt for war,' suggesting the 'heroes' are fighting men and the 'makers of war' the weapon-forging craftsmen.
Gill takes the figures as the particulars of the ten thousand, seven thousand being the principal men of the land.

debates.question: How is the total of ten thousand squared with the 3,023 of Jer. 52:28?

sides.position: a scribal confusion of a yod (ten) with a gimel (three)

themes: a city left incapable of war

schools.summary: Ellicott traces Mattaniah's pedigree and first claim as Jehoiachin's uncle, and hears in the new name Zedekiah ('Jah is righteousness') the delusive hopes of Jer. 23:1-9; Keil gives his age and standing.
Clarke identifies Mattaniah as Josiah's son and Jehoiakim's brother; Wesley reads the imposed name as a warning of the divine justice that would overtake Zedekiah's own perfidy.
Gill identifies Mattaniah as the third son of Josiah.
Henry makes the changed name a token of servitude — 'they have set up kings, but not by me' (Hos. 8:4) — and a presage of God's justice glorified in his ruin.

themes: kings set up not by God

schools.summary: Keil names Hamutal of Libnah and shows from the close parallel in Jeremiah 52 — with its omissions and additions — that neither text was borrowed from the other but both from a common source.
Gill reckons that Zedekiah was but nine or ten when Josiah died, the whole sequence of his brothers' reigns compressed into a decade.

Ellicott sets the verdict beside Jeremiah's bad figs and Ezekiel's denunciation of Zedekiah's broken faith; Keil insists God did not make Zedekiah wicked, but such a God-resisting man became king to Jerusalem's ruin.
Clarke marvels that not one of these kings takes warning from the judgments that fell on his predecessors.

themes: not God but the man's own evil

schools.summary: Clarke fixes the revolt in Zedekiah's eighth year and reproves the broken oath as the filling up of iniquity; Wesley reads the people's own sins as the true cause why God gave and suffered wicked kings.

Ellicott parses the syntax ('upon the anger of Jehovah it befell Jerusalem'), puts a full stop before 'And Zedekiah rebelled,' and details the doomed coalition with Egypt and the neighbouring peoples that Jeremiah opposed.
